item.title: Mastra npm Compromise, RoguePlanet CVE-2026-50656, and AI Tool Data Theft
item.description: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender...
item.content:encoded:
This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege zero-day and a confirmed Kodak data breach claimed by ShinyHunters. Multiple reports from Aikido, Socket, and The Hacker News describe a coordinated npm supply chain attack affecting more than 140 packages in the @mastra namespace. The malicious publications occurred on June 17, when a hijacked npm account belonging to a legitimate former Mastra contributor mass-published compromised versions across the scope within a short window. The affected packages reportedly did not embed malware directly, but instead added a dependency on easy-day-js, a malicious clone or typosquat of dayjs. The easy-day-js package used a postinstall hook to execute an obfuscated setup script during installation. That script downloaded a second-stage payload from attacker-controlled infrastructure, including 23[.]254[.]164[.]92:8000, wrote it to a randomly named JavaScript file in the operating system temporary directory, spawned it as a detached background process, and deleted itself. Socket reported that the loader also disabled TLS certificate validation before retrieving the payload. The recovered second-stage malware was described as a cross-platform Node.js tasking client with persistence mechanisms for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Its collection logic targeted host reconnaissance data, browser history from Chrome, Edge, and Brave, and more than 160 cryptocurrency wallet browser extensions. Researchers noted similarities to an earlier axios compromise, including the use of a malicious dependency, a clean decoy version preceding the malicious release, automatic postinstall execution, and self-deleting droppers. Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins masquerading as AI coding assistants. The plugins reportedly functioned as advertised while secretly exfiltrating AI provider API keys entered by users, including keys for OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek. The stolen keys were sent over plaintext HTTP to 39.107.60[.]51. Two of the reported JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, were listed with more than 25,000 downloads each, although the authenticity of those counts was unclear. The campaign reflects continued attacker interest in developer environments where AI service credentials may be stored, entered, or reused across workflows. Separately, researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, captured users’ conversations with AI chatbots. The extensions reportedly provided genuine ad-blocking capability while operating an undisclosed telemetry channel that collected full AI conversation histories, model usage, and subscription tier metadata across services including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI. Microsoft confirmed it is working on a security update for a Microsoft Defender zero-day vulnerability publicly referred to as RoguePlanet and now tracked as CVE-2026-50656. The issue is described as an elevation-of-privilege flaw in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine involving a race condition. Researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept exploit in a self-hosted Git repository and claimed the exploit affects fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems. According to the reporting, the exploit can spawn command prompts with SYSTEM privileges and may work regardless of whether Defender real-time protection is enabled. The disclosure is also tied to an ongoing dispute between the researcher and Microsoft regarding bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure practices. Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed a limited amount of company data. The company said it engaged external cybersecurity experts, is investigating what data was accessed and copied, and is working with law enforcement. Kodak also stated that it is confident there is no threat to its systems or operations. The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed responsibility for the incident on its dark web leak site. The group alleged it stole more than 2.2 million records containing customer personally identifiable information and internal corporate data, and threatened to leak the data on June 18, 2026.

144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account — Must Read

Attackers hijacked a former Mastra contributor’s npm account and pushed malicious versions of 144 @mastra packages that added the easy-day-js dependency, triggering an install-time stealer via postinstall. Any systems that installed the affected versions should be treated as potentially compromised, with credentials rotated and hosts audited for persistence.

  • As many as 144 npm packages associated with the Mastra namespace were compromised in a software supply chain attack codenamed easy-day-js.
  • A hijacked npm account belonging to a legitimate former Mastra contributor mass-published more than 140 malicious packages across the Mastra scope within about 88 minutes.
  • The compromised Mastra packages did not contain malicious code directly but added a third-party dependency called easy-day-js, which launched an obfuscated postinstall payload.
  • The final-stage malware could harvest browser history and data from more than 160 cryptocurrency wallet browser extensions, establish persistence on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and exfiltrate data to a command-and-control server.
  • Any workstation, CI runner, or build environment that installed the affected versions should be treated as potentially compromised, rolled back to a safe version, audited, and have credentials rotated.

Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats — Should Read

Researchers found 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants that steal users’ AI provider API keys, likely to resell access for LLMjacking. Separately, two Chrome ad blocker extensions were caught secretly collecting users’ private conversations with major AI chatbots.

  • Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins that pose as AI coding assistants and exfiltrate AI provider API keys.
  • The malicious JetBrains plugins function as advertised but secretly send entered API keys for providers such as OpenAI, SiliconFlow, or DeepSeek to the attacker-controlled server 39.107.60[.]51 over plaintext HTTP.
  • Two of the malicious JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, have more than 25,000 downloads each, though the article says it is unclear whether the counts are authentic or inflated.
  • Researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, capture users’ conversations with AI chatbots including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI.
  • The Chrome extensions provide genuine ad-blocking functionality while running an undisclosed telemetry channel that collects full AI conversation history, model usage, and subscription tier metadata.

Microsoft working on Defender patch for RoguePlanet zero-day — Should Read

Microsoft is working on a patch for the publicly disclosed RoguePlanet zero-day in Microsoft Defender, now tracked as CVE-2026-50656, which can allow attackers to gain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows 10 and 11 systems. The flaw was released amid an ongoing dispute between researcher Nightmare Eclipse and Microsoft over vulnerability disclosure practices.

  • Microsoft confirmed it is working on a security patch for the Microsoft Defender zero-day vulnerability named RoguePlanet, which is now tracked as CVE-2026-50656.
  • The RoguePlanet exploit reportedly affects fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 devices and allows attackers to spawn command prompts with SYSTEM privileges through a Microsoft Defender race condition.
  • The researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept exploit for RoguePlanet in a self-hosted Git repository and said the exploit can work regardless of whether real-time protection is enabled.
  • Microsoft said it is aware of an elevation of privilege issue in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine publicly referred to as RoguePlanet and will update the CVE when a security update is available.
  • The RoguePlanet disclosure is part of an ongoing dispute between Nightmare Eclipse and Microsoft over bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure practices.

Kodak confirms data breach claimed by ShinyHunters extortion gang — May Read

Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed and copied a limited amount of company data, while the ShinyHunters extortion gang claims it stole over 2.2 million records containing customer PII and internal corporate data. Kodak says it is investigating with external cybersecurity experts and law enforcement and that its systems and operations are not currently threatened.

  • Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party illegally gained temporary access to a limited amount of company data.
  • Kodak said it engaged external cybersecurity experts to investigate what data was accessed and copied and is working with law enforcement.
  • Kodak said it is confident there is no threat to its systems or operations.
  • The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed responsibility for the breach on its dark web leak site.
  • ShinyHunters alleged it stole over 2.2 million records containing customer personally identifiable information and internal corporate data and threatened to leak the data on June 18, 2026.

item.title: Mastra npm Supply Chain Attack Backdoors 140+ Packages via easy-day-js
item.description: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the @mastra npm organization, exposing downstream development and CI/CD environments to a postinstall-based payload chain. The incident is notable for its scale, its targeting of an AI framework package namespace, and its use of a staged delivery mechanism designed to execute during package installation. The affected Mastra packages reportedly had combined weekly downloads exceeding 1.1 million, making this a high-impact software supply chain event for organizations consuming JavaScript and AI development dependencies. StepSecurity reported that on June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added a dependency named easy-day-js across more than 140 Mastra packages. The package name closely resembled the legitimate dayjs library, indicating a typosquatting approach intended to blend into JavaScript dependency trees and exploit developer familiarity with common date/time libraries. The malicious package was staged across versions. Version 1.11.21 of easy-day-js was reportedly published as a clean bait package, while version 1.11.22 introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper. This sequencing is consistent with supply chain tradecraft in which an attacker first establishes a benign-looking package history before pushing a malicious update after trust or dependency inclusion has been achieved. The reported malicious behavior centered on npm’s postinstall lifecycle hook. According to StepSecurity, the obfuscated script attempted to download a second-stage payload from https://23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878, spawn it as a detached Node.js process, and then delete setup.cjs after execution. This design indicates an effort to move beyond package-level code execution into a more persistent or independently running payload while reducing local forensic artifacts tied to the installer script. The use of a direct IP address and nonstandard port for payload retrieval suggests attacker-controlled infrastructure rather than a commodity package registry-hosted payload. StepSecurity also reported that, during a controlled GitHub Actions test, its Harden Runner in block mode intercepted and blocked the outbound connection to 23.254.164.92:8000, preventing the stage-two payload from being retrieved in that environment. The reported scale of the compromised package set places this incident among high-concern npm ecosystem supply chain events, particularly because it affected an AI framework namespace with broad downstream usage. StepSecurity stated that the affected Mastra packages had more than 1.1 million combined weekly downloads, and assessed that anyone who installed an @mastra package on the day of the compromise should consider the environment compromised. For threat hunters and detection engineers, the reporting provides concrete infrastructure and behavioral details: the package name easy-day-js, malicious version 1.11.22, postinstall execution, retrieval from 23.254.164.92:8000, and execution of a detached Node.js second stage. The incident reinforces ongoing adversary interest in developer ecosystems, CI/CD workflows, and trusted open-source dependency chains as distribution mechanisms for code execution at scale.

item.title: npm shai_hulululud Targets AI Malware Scanners With Prompt Injection
item.description: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package,...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package, shaihulululud@1.0.48596, uses prompt-injection-style content, context flooding, and layered obfuscation to complicate automated review. Socket Threat Research identified shaihulululud@1.0.48596 as a suspicious npm package that differs from recent Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades activity. Rather than carrying the credential theft behavior associated with those campaigns, the package appears aimed at disrupting AI-based malware scanners and analysis workflows. The package includes an approximately 9.28 MB index.js file containing policy-triggering prompt text, fake system override instructions, and tens of thousands of repeated comment lines. This material is not executed by JavaScript, but it can influence LLM-based scanners by consuming context windows, causing truncation, triggering refusals, or shifting attention away from executable logic. Socket also reported that executable JavaScript was appended after the large prompt and comment content. Researchers described the code as obfuscated through a character-code array, a ROT-style substitution function, eval usage, and a second AES-encrypted layer, and classified the package as protestware or potentially unwanted behavior. The report highlights a growing concern around adversarial content embedded in software packages to manipulate AI-enabled security tooling. In this case, the malicious or unwanted behavior is less about exploiting runtime environments directly and more about shaping how automated scanners interpret, summarize, or fail to analyze the package. The technique illustrates how non-executed source content can still have operational impact when analysis pipelines rely on large language models. Prompt-like instructions and repeated filler can create conditions where analysis output is incomplete, refused, or biased toward irrelevant text while executable code remains buried near the end of the file. This finding is notable for threat hunters, detection engineers, and SOC analysts tracking software supply chain abuse because it reflects a shift in attacker experimentation toward the analysis layer itself. The package demonstrates how npm ecosystem threats may increasingly combine traditional JavaScript obfuscation with content designed specifically to degrade AI-assisted triage and malware classification.

item.title: JetBrains AI Key Theft, Vertex AI Bucket Squatting, Rokarolla, and REDCap Exposure
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed research infrastructure linked to espionage activity. Aikido Security and BleepingComputer reported a coordinated campaign involving at least 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants, code-review tools, and Git utilities. The plugins exfiltrated AI provider API keys entered into their settings to a hardcoded server at 39.107.60[.]51 over plain HTTP, while continuing to function as advertised. The plugins were published under seven vendor accounts and had nearly 70,000 reported installs, though Aikido noted that marketplace download counts and reviews may have been manipulated. The campaign reflects broader concern over developer machine compromise and package ecosystem abuse. StepSecurity described recent npm and PyPI attacks such as Miasma and Hades that install malicious package versions on developer workstations and harvest secrets, including registry publishing tokens. Its analysis focused on package-manager configuration files such as .npmrc, bunfig.toml, .yarnrc, .yarnrc.yml, and pip.conf as locations where effective registry settings and hardcoded credentials can expose developers and maintainers. A separate AI platform issue affected the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python, where predictable default staging bucket names enabled a bucket-squatting attack against model uploads. Unit 42 showed that an attacker with a victim project ID and region could pre-create the expected Cloud Storage bucket, receive uploaded model files, and replace them with a malicious pickle or joblib model before Vertex AI loaded it. Google addressed the issue by adding randomness to bucket names in version 1.144.0 and ownership verification in version 1.148.0. Netskope published analysis arguing that zero trust strategies are expanding beyond human users and workloads to include AI agents. The article cited Anthropic’s “Zero Trust for AI Agents” framing and emphasized agent identity as a control point for determining which agents can access which resources under specific conditions. The piece described blended identity models that combine an agent’s identity with the identity of the human invoking it, using contextual attributes such as time, location, sensitivity, risk score, and application instance. It also framed prompt injection, jailbreaking, anomalous behavior, and data exfiltration as security concerns for agent inputs and outputs. Zimperium reported a new Android banking trojan named Rokarolla targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications with 137 supported commands. The malware is distributed through malicious websites impersonating Google Chrome or TikTok downloads, then presents itself as Google Play Protect during installation. Once launched, it requests Accessibility service access along with notification, SMS, and call permissions, and uses fake overlays to steal credentials, card data, and other financial information. Kaspersky also identified malware distribution through Steam Workshop content for the Wallpaper Engine application. Malicious “application wallpapers,” which are executable Windows applications, were downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times before being reported. Observed payloads included DarkKomet, Lumma, Vidar, cryptocurrency miners, botnet loaders, RanEngine, and ransomware strains, with Steam removing the reported items after notification. Censys published an exposure analysis of REDCap following Google Threat Intelligence Group reporting on UNC6508, a PRC-nexus actor linked to a year-plus espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions. UNC6508 reportedly exploited an externally facing REDCap server, deployed a webshell, and used the INFINITERED PHP backdoor, although the initial access method remains unconfirmed. Censys observed just over 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap instances globally as of June 16, 2026, with 40% in the United States and limited adoption of the latest observed version. Health-ISAC separately reported that cyber insurance policyholders are facing heavier scrutiny in underwriting and claims despite declining rates. The article cited insurer concern over concentration in large U.S. policyholders and the potential for a major supply chain event or outage to create systemic impact. It also noted that organizations are examining continuity scenarios involving major IT outages caused by malicious activity or flawed updates.

item.title: CVE-2026-27577 n8n RCE, GhostTree Evasion, and AI Security Governance
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to address abuse, fraud, and child safety concerns, raising parallel security and privacy questions. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-27577, a critical n8n code injection vulnerability that allows an authenticated user with workflow creation or editing permissions to execute system commands on the host through crafted workflow expressions. The flaw stems from a sandbox escape in n8n’s expression and code-node evaluation environment, involving PrototypeSanitizer behavior that can expose host process internals. Affected versions include n8n before 1.123.22, versions 2.0.0 through 2.9.2, and 2.10.0 across self-hosted and n8n Cloud deployments, with fixes released on February 25, 2026. Varonis described GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless valid file path combinations. Because users with write access can create junctions without administrator privileges, GhostBranch and GhostTree structures can cause recursive scanners, including EDR products, to follow loops and fail to complete inspection of target folders. The report states that Microsoft later patched a Windows Defender bypass after initially closing the issue. Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to surface supply chain risk in project manifests, particularly missing lockfiles that leave dependency resolution unpinned. The feature was built after customer exposure analysis during the Axios npm compromise showed that risk was not limited to projects directly depending on the compromised Axios version. Socket separates these alerts from dependency alerts and reports the affected manifest, package-manager context, and impact details. Health-ISAC covered the FDA’s draft guidance for artificial intelligence-enabled medical devices, emphasizing how regulators are adapting to software that changes over time. The non-binding 2025 guidance calls out AI-specific risks including model drift, bias, and data poisoning, while expecting manufacturers to use predetermined change-control plans that preserve monitoring, testing, transparency, and auditability. Phil Englert of Health-ISAC framed the approach as recognition of AI’s evolving nature while maintaining accountability and patient safety. Censys described its integration with Google SecOps, positioning internet intelligence as enrichment for AI-enabled security operations workflows. The integration can add context about IPs, web properties, certificates, active DNS, historical observations, adversary clusters, and infrastructure relationships directly into SecOps cases and SOAR flows. The reporting emphasizes analyst pivoting from single indicators into related infrastructure and using historical lookups or rescans to understand how assets appeared during an incident window. NetSPI warned that regulated industries evaluating AI security tooling face practical constraints around determinism, auditability, trust boundaries, cost predictability, and evidentiary consistency. The article argues that many LLM-enabled security systems remain better suited as productivity enhancers for skilled practitioners than as replacements for human judgment. It also highlights budgeting uncertainty from token-based pricing and the potential mismatch between non-deterministic AI outputs and auditor or regulator expectations. The Record reported that India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of the June 21 NEET-UG medical entrance exam rerun due to concerns the platform could be used for cheating. Authorities also directed Telegram to disable message editing in India until June 30, citing scams in which edited messages made fake leaked exam questions appear authentic after the fact. The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed many Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent exam paper access, while Ahmedabad police arrested members of a cyber fraud ring accused of operating eight Telegram channels and moving about 15 million rupees through fraudulent bank accounts. BleepingComputer reported that the UK plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations expected before Christmas and implementation anticipated in spring 2027. Covered platforms are expected to include user-to-user services with algorithmic feeds such as Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook, and X, while messaging services and YouTube Kids are excluded. New accounts may require age verification through identity document uploads or facial age scans, and experts cited in the article warned that these mechanisms can be bypassed while creating risks tied to biometric and identity data collection.

item.title: Rokarolla, FortiSandbox CVEs, DragonForce Teams Relays, and LiteSpeed cPanel Exploitation
item.description: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure,...
item.content:encoded: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure, and trusted communication channels into intrusion workflows, complicating detection and attribution. Mobile banking malware, ransomware operations, phishing, cloud audit fidelity, and exploited appliance flaws all featured prominently. The coverage also reflected growing attention to continuous controls validation, operational resilience in healthcare, and AI-enabled analysis in OT environments. CISA added an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability, identified in the reporting as CVE-2026-48172, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. The flaw affects LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8 and reportedly allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected systems within three days under BOD 26-04 guidance. Fortinet FortiSandbox also appeared in exploitation reporting, with Defused Cyber observing activity against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089. The first two issues involve JRPC API path traversal and OS command injection, both potentially reachable through crafted HTTP requests by unauthenticated attackers. CVE-2026-25089 was patched more recently, and Defused Cyber assessed that the observed exploit attempt for that flaw appeared AI-generated and faulty, with no working public exploit known. Zimperium documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with support for 137 remote commands. The malware is distributed through malicious sites impersonating popular applications such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper posing as Google Play Protect. Once installed, it abuses Accessibility permissions to disable protections, capture lock-screen credentials, deploy fake login overlays, read and send SMS messages, log screens and keystrokes, and silently replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. DragonForce ransomware was reported abusing Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure through a custom Go-based backdoor named Backdoor.Turn. Symantec described it as the first known in-the-wild malware to use Teams relay servers to mask command-and-control traffic. In a December 2025 intrusion against a major U.S. services company, the attackers likely gained access through an unknown SQL or MSSQL server flaw, used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers to obtain kernel-level privileges, exfiltrated data, and deployed ransomware. Business email compromise reporting from IRONSCALES showed a same-day payment request sent from a legitimate construction consulting domain via hosted Exchange infrastructure. SPF passed and DMARC returned bestguesspass, while the body fabricated a prior thread using real employee names and attached a clean PDF invoice with Wells Fargo banking details. The x-originating-ip header pointed to 45[.]67[.]97[.]6 in Seoul, inconsistent with the Albuquerque-based sender, and the message was mitigated before recipient action. A Spur Intelligence survey of more than 200 practitioners found anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, present in nearly every security incident. Nearly half of surveyed organizations reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted through these services. Respondents also cited lack of IP context as a major analysis challenge, with many teams still using IP intelligence primarily after alerts are generated. Permiso highlighted risks around GCP’s deprecated serviceData field and the broader dependency of cloud detection engineering on audit log quality. The article emphasized that administrative activity in GCP is represented through audit log entries and that their structure and completeness directly affect detection rules, investigations, and post-incident reconstruction. The reporting framed log fidelity as a core constraint on reliable cloud threat detection. Tenable and XM Cyber coverage reflected the continued movement from traditional vulnerability counting toward continuous threat exposure management and attack-path prioritization. Tenable One’s continuous control validation maps defenses such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, SIEM, and DLP onto potential attack paths, while XM Cyber’s SC Awards Europe win was tied to attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and identification of choke points where a single remediation can disrupt multiple routes to critical assets. Radiflow described AI’s dual role in OT security: a force multiplier for attackers through deepfakes, automated phishing, faster scanning, and vulnerability chaining, and a defensive aid for asset visibility, threat intelligence, prioritization, and SOC efficiency. Its reporting focused on passive OT network learning through iSID and digital-twin-based breach-and-attack simulation through CIARA. In healthcare, Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego CISO Sahan Fernando emphasized operational engagement as central to cyber resilience and continuity of patient care, citing sector-wide collaboration through Health-ISAC, the Health Sector Coordinating Council, and Operation Vital Signs.

item.title: FortiSandbox, n8n, Cisco SD-WAN Exploitation and APT37 NarwhalRAT Activity
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally...
item.content:encoded: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally reachable workflow platforms, alongside regulatory pressure to produce incident context quickly. Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws were reported as under active exploitation, with Defused observing attacks against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in the prior 24 hours. Fortinet had issued fixes for the three critical-severity vulnerabilities on April 14; the flaws allow unauthenticated attackers to escalate privileges and execute unauthorized code via low-complexity command injection without user interaction. Fortinet also previously flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal issue, as exploited in the wild. Cisco released updates for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw stems from insufficient validation during file upload and can allow an authenticated remote attacker with write access to create or overwrite files, potentially escalating privileges to root. CISA added the issue to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a June 29, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cyera disclosed CVE-2026-21858, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability affecting locally deployed n8n instances. The issue arises from Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks, allowing attacker-controlled JSON to override trusted file metadata and copy local files such as n8n’s SQLite database or configuration into retrievable workflow data. The researchers described how those files could enable forged administrator sessions and subsequent command execution through n8n’s Execute Command node. CyCognito reported expanding external attack surface analysis to more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton. The company described cases involving an exposed MCP server permitting unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data and a public CrewAI stack where the agent API was authenticated but the RAG knowledge base remained exposed. Silent Push separately framed NIS2’s 24-hour early warning requirement as dependent on rapid infrastructure context, arguing that DNS, WHOIS, certificate, host scan, traffic, and behavioral relationship data are increasingly central to timely incident characterization. ESET reported Windows variants of SprySOCKS, previously associated with Linux malware, used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The activity was attributed with high confidence to Earth Lusca, also tracked as FishMonger and known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22. The Windows variants, WINDRV and WINPLUS, support TCP, UDP, and WebSocket communications, more than 30 C2 commands, SOCKS proxying, process and file management, and collection of keystrokes, clipboard contents, and active window titles. Separately, Genians reported that North Korean group ScarCruft, or APT37, used spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security alerts to deliver NarwhalRAT. The emails referenced abnormal one-time password activity and carried ZIP archives containing malicious LNK files, which initiated a multi-stage infection chain involving batch scripts, Python retrieval, and scheduled-task persistence. NarwhalRAT supports keystroke logging, screenshots, ambient audio recording, USB data collection, directory uploads, C2 command execution, and C2 switching via Korean website relays and pCloud API functionality. Kaspersky reported malicious Wallpaper Engine packages distributed through Steam Workshop since late 2025. The campaign abused Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds, and some malicious wallpapers had thousands or tens of thousands of downloads. One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe, used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate and hijack live Steam sessions, and exfiltrated data to 120.48.156[.]17. iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach involving patient personal and health information stored in third-party-hosted business applications. The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claimed possession of sensitive data, and demanded payment to prevent disclosure. iRhythm attributed access to social engineering and said it had no evidence that products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution, or financial reporting systems were affected.
